{{Chess Position |Sicilian defence |eco=[[Chess/ECOB|B27-B99]] |parent=[[Chess/Sicilian Defence|Sicilian Defence]] }} == 2. Nf3 == White increases their control of the d4 square: the conventional plan is to follow with 3. d4 cxd4 4. Nxd4 and open the position. Black's attention must now turn to their development: determining how to release their bishops, or first developing their knight. The main lines are '''2...d6''', accounting for almost half of games, and '''2...Nc6''' and '''2...e6''', each about half as popular as 2...d6. === Release a bishop === At some point Black would like to do something with their bishops, and that requires they make a pawn move first. [[/2...d6|'''2...d6''']] is the '''main line'''. This releases the queen's bishop on the c8-h3 diagonal, and because of the pawn on d6, it usually means the king's bishop must fianchetto to g7. 2...d6 also controls e5, ensuring Black can play ...Nf6. After 3. d4 cxd4 4. Nxd4 this usually leads to the Najdorf, Dragon, classical, or Scheveningen Sicilians. A sideline is 3. Bb5+, the Moscow variation. [[/2...e6|'''2...e6''']], sometimes called the '''French Sicilian''', opens the a3-f8 diagonal for the king's bishop, and since the e6 pawn obstructs the queen's bishop, in these lines it is usually fianchettoed to b7. The typical ...e6 Sicilians are the Kan, Taimanov, or Four Knights variations. This can also transpose to the Scheveningen with ...d6. Preparing to fianchetto straight away is less common. [[/2...g6|'''2...g6''']], preparing ...Bg7, is the '''hyper-accelerated dragon Sicilian''' (''accelerated'' over the regular dragon Sicilian in that Black hasn't played ...d6 yet, and ''hyper-''accelerated in that Black hasn't played ...Nc6 yet either). [[/2...b6|'''2...b6!?''']] to prepare Bb7, called the '''Katalimov''', is non-standard. It may transpose into a Kan or other ...e6 Sicilian, though Black will have lost a tempo if they intend to play ...a6 and ...b5. === Develop a knight === [[/2...Nc6|'''2...Nc6''']] is the most traditional move, sometimes called the '''old Sicilian'''. Black develops the knight to its natural square. It reserves options in the [[w:en:Center (chess)#Centre/Center|centre]] and keeps an eye on d4. After 3. d4 cxd4 4. Nxd4 this usually leads to the Sveshinkov, Accelerated Dragon or Kalashnikov Sicilians. However, this allows White to enter the sideline 3. Bb5, the Rossolimo attack. The most significant departure from the above main lines is [[/2...Nf6|'''2...Nf6''']], the '''Nimzowitsch variation'''. As in [[Chess Opening Theory/1. e4/1...Nf6|Alekhine's defence]], Black attacks the e-pawn to tempt it forward. === Rare move orders=== [[/2...a6|'''2...a6''']] is the '''O'Kelly variation'''. ...a6 is played in many Open Sicilian lines eventually to control the b6 square. This move order creates some novel possibilities, e.g. after 3. d4 cxd4 4. Nxd4 Nf6 5. Nc3 e5! White's knight cannot go to b5, so must retreat, and as Black hasn't played ...d6 yet (as in the Najdorf) they can play ...Bb4!. It can restrain some of White's longer-term plans and provide the springboard for ...b5. Another move-order anomaly is [[/2...Qc7|'''2...Qc7''']] the '''Quinetros variation'''. Black's queen ends up on c7 in many Sicilians and this can transpose, e.g. 3. d4 cxd4 4. Nxd4 a6 5. Nc3 e6 transposes to the Kan Sicilian. === Bad moves === [[/2...e5|'''2...e5?''']] is the '''Jalalabad variation''' where Black immediately throws away their e-pawn. [[/2...b5|'''2...b5?''']] is the '''Polish gambit'''. Black gives up their b-pawn to 3. Bxb5. ==Theory table== {{Chess Opening Theory/Table}} '''1. e4 c5 2. Nf3'''
